issue_title: UWSGI process fails to respond after Harakiri/respawn
issue_body:
We run a django application under UWSGI.  We use harakiri to protect against stuck processes.  Any request that takes longer than 60 seconds will trigger a Harakiri in the worker process and the master uwsgi process will spawn a new child process.  This works very well when datadog is not installed.

When datadog is installed, however, uwsgi starts a new child process but that newly-spawned process never responds to any more requests.

Attached is an example from our logs.  Look for the word DAMN.  Please notice, activity for the new worker process that is started is absent from the log.  
[web1.log.txt](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/files/2102893/web1.log.txt)

After a while, this happens again, and then there are no more responding workers and the server becomes inaccessible for web requests.

If we remove datadog, we do not experience this problem.

Also attached is the configuration file we use for UWSGI. 
[django.ini.txt](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/files/2102943/django.ini.txt)

What could be going wrong?

I'm curious if other people would be interested in having examples in this fashion: https://github.com/enlitic/lasagne4newbs/blob/master/mnist_conv.py. I've found the existing examples to be a little confusing to people new to Lasagne, and made that version to help them out. I think the biggest downside would be teaching slightly less than optimal practices (specifically not transfering a large amount of input data to the GPU at once).

`search` should be called `get_events` and it should take `headers` *or* `start_time`, `end_time` as arguments.

A new function `search_runs` should pass through to MDS `find_header`.

Hey!

Thanks for this awesome Pytorch project! I'm totally sold on the idea, and wanted to take a crack and getting involved! 

I found recently that for language model training where the output vocabulary space is high-dimensional, that Adaptive Softmax from (https://arxiv.org/abs/1609.04309) can lead to a huge speed up. It's essentially a modification of hierarchical softmax that is optimized for GPU computing, and it can sometimes lead to orders of magnitude speed improvements. I figured that since this would make seq2seq decoding a lot faster, it'd perhaps be useful, not to mention a good way for me to dive in and find my way around. Does that sound helpful? If not a have a few other features I want to implement so I could propose something else. 

If that sounds good, I got the code from this repo: https://github.com/rosinality/adaptive-softmax-pytorch, to work with the word_language_model example in Pytorch, and thought it'd be a good place to start. I figured that it'd make sense to implement the output projection, activation, and loss in an nn module, and then add an option for seq2seq training to use this activation and loss rather than the normal seq2seq and crossentropy. Does that sound good?

Also I noticed in the seq2seq training file that the decoder is hardcoded as an LSTM, but is intended to implemented differently by @pdasigi. If you don't mind my asking, what's the plan for that? I want to use the Transformer seq2seq which requires changing the decoder.

issue_title: Stop telling devs to run certbot_test against staging
issue_body:
@sydneyli brought to my attention that the way we're telling developers to use `certbot_test` in https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#running-a-local-copy-of-the-client does not work. You cannot use `certbot_test` against the staging server because `certbot_test` uses alternate ports for challenges which isn't supported in staging.

In my opinion, we should remove the recommendation of using `certbot_test` entirely and tell people to run the client as root with the staging flag.

issue_title: nbextension install output should go to stdout
issue_body:
The refactor in 4.2 switched to using the logging module for nbextension install, but this goes to stderr by default. We shouldn't write anything to stderr when installing or uninstalling an extension without an error.

In general, I think using the application logger is probably not the right thing to do for most of the output in nbextension/serverextension actions, and we should be using print to stdout, stderr instead.

issue_title: Support setting metadata like title, author and date
issue_body:
# Problem

Currently there is no way on the UI to set metadata, such as title, author and date, of a notebook.  In consequence, one has to enter such information in the main document, or dedicate one slide to it, using heading 1 for title, sub-headings or paragraphs for author and date.  Trouble is, afterwards when s/he wants to convert the notebook to another format, especially PDF, such metadata become part of the main content.  In short, the input (notebook) and output (PDF) format are not well aligned.

# Suggestion

Support setting metadata like title, author and date in the UI and use them to produce the counterpart in the output format when `nbconvert`-ing the notebook, so that a document will have a head with  title/author/date and slides will have a cover with similarly information.

import asyncio

issue_body: The notebook format can already include images etc. via `"output_type" : "display_data"` cells. However, there is currently no UI to add such cells to a notebook. As a result, if you want an image in a notebook then you need to either (a) write a code cell to read and display it (b) link to an external image file that you must then carry around with the notebook, or (c) insert an `

Question: is it possible to theme or visually enhance the dashboards.
I actually like the visual style in the dashboard layout where each cell is visually separated. 
This disappears when in "dashboard preview" and all the cells merge visually, having the
same cell-background color as the background color of the whole page.

Many thanks to all the dashboard developers!

issue_title: Rework the way we initialize an application
issue_body:
Almost everywhere we initialize an application, we end up doing:

```python
app = MyApp().init()
```

I'm not sure I see any reason to have the class instantiation separated from the work we do in `.init().`  Furthermore, because `.testing_prep()` and `.cli_run()` instantiate the app "inside" their helper objects, it's not possible to send constructor arguments through in those situations.  Effectively, anything that could be configured from passing things in to `Flask(...)` become impossible in CLI and Testing scenarios.  That's not bit us in the past, but it's a big oversight in the init process.

issue_title: Add example of two-way TLS/SSL configuration
issue_body:

> If you use these parameters, don't use the built-in `cert` parameter of `requests` at the same time. 

I thought that the `verify` field shouldn't be used as well, which resulted in lots of attempts which didn't happen to work.

> This doesn't mean that You can't use the `verify` field, which *should* be used for two-way SSL. Check out the [requests library documentation for the `verify` field](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#ssl-cert-verification).

issue_title: Please document difference between BleachSanitizerFilter and html5lib’s sanitizer.Filter
issue_body:
It’s not clear what the difference is between using Bleach’s sanitizer and the one which ships with html5lib.

`BleachSanitizerFilter` inherits from `html5lib.filters.sanitizer.Filter` and overrides the `sanitize_token()`, `allow_token()` and `sanitize_css()` methods, but even reading the code isn’t entirely obvious what the functional difference is.

It does have a slightly different API and different defaults — are they the only differences? Or given equivalent config (tags, attributes, etc), would they give the same output?

If not, in what way(s) is Bleach’s version better?

issue_title: Allow default resampling options based on dataset metadata
issue_body:
Similar to how enhancements work (or should work) the users should be able to do `scn.resample('area')` without any resampling parameters and have SatPy load a configuration file to determine what resampling would be best. In some cases it may be possible to have the resampling be smart about choosing the parameters dynamically. For example, choose nearest neighbor search radius based on nadir or limb resolutions of a swath. Or even more obvious, if defaulting to non-nearest resampling then make sure to not interpolate category data (`flag_meanings` in metadata).

As mentioned this should be similar to enhancements where there can be a default resampling section, otherwise the best options could be selected by metadata like `standard_name` or `name` or `units`.

```

...

test_nearest_resize (pyresample.test.test_image.Test) ... 
```

Please note that the test seems to pass regularly with python 2.7.

issue_title: Importing tflearn is slow due to tf.contrib imports
issue_body:
Hey 👋 

[Someone over at Stack Overflow was wondering](https://stackoverflow.com/q/51061689/51685) about the time it takes to import `tflearn` compared to other modules (4.3 seconds for `tflearn`, 1.1 seconds for `tensorflow` in my example container).
In my SO answer I sleuthed around a bit, and it looks like the brunt of the import time comes from [`tflearn.variables` importing `tensorflow.contrib` for the `add_arg_scope` decorator](https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/8aa8436a641f019983a4d5ea2290ba98eed8a181/tflearn/variables.py#L7).

Since the `arg_scope` module isn't too complex, maybe it would be worth it to vendor it in `tflearn` to avoid the added import time?

Other places within tflearn that import `tf.contrib`, as of 8aa8436a641f019983, seem to be:

```
$ git grep tensorflow.contrib tflearn/
tflearn/data_utils.py:    from tensorflow.contrib.learn.python.learn.preprocessing.text import \
tflearn/estimators/cluster/kmeans.py:from tensorflow.contrib.factorization.python.ops import clustering_ops as c_ops
tflearn/estimators/cluster/kmeans.py:from tensorflow.contrib.tensor_forest.python.ops import data_ops

tflearn/initializations.py:    from tensorflow.contrib.layers.python.layers.initializers import \

tflearn/layers/recurrent.py:    from tensorflow.contrib.rnn.python.ops.core_rnn import static_rnn as _rnn, static_bidirectional_rnn as _brnn

Of these, it looks like `tflearn.variables`, `tflearn.initializations` and `tflearn.layers.recurrent` are always imported, so it might be worth it to look into lazily importing the `tf.contrib` dependencies in them?

issue_title: get-unsat-assumptions behaving incorrectly when :produce-unsat-cores is present
issue_body:
There seems to be an unintended interaction between `get-unsat-assumptions` and `:produce-unsat-cores`.

Consider this:

```
(set-logic QF_BV)
(set-option :produce-unsat-assumptions true)
; (set-option :produce-unsat-cores true)
(declare-const s0 Bool)
(assert (! false :named |absurd|))
(check-sat-assuming (s0))
(get-unsat-assumptions)
```

Note the commented out line for producing unsat cores, on line 3. Z3 *correctly* produces the following response:

```
unsat
()
```

The subset is empty because the constant `s0` really has nothing to do with the unsatisfiability of the problem. This is perfectly fine.

However, if I uncomment line 3 leaving everything else the same, z3 says:

```
unsat
(absurd)
```

Strictly speaking this is incorrect, since `get-unsat-assumptions` should always return a subset of the literals specified in `check-sat-assumptions` according to the spec:

> (get-unsat-assumptions) returns a subset a1, . . . , an of the assumptions in the most recent
> check-sat-assuming command. These assumptions are such that issuing the command
> (check-sat-assuming (a1 · · · an)) instead would have still produced an unsat response.
> The returned sequence is not required to be minimal.

Clearly, `(absurd)` is not a subset of `(s0)`. So this behavior does not seem to be SMTLib compliant.

issue_title: Z3 SMTLIB parser does not recognise `fp.to_ieee_bv`.

[x.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/825193/x.smt2.txt) is produced by calling `Z3_solver_to_string()` inside a tool I am working on. In many cases the output (when a `(check-sat)` call is appended) produces a query that Z3 can parse.

`async with session.get(url ,payload={'mode':'fastest;car;traffic:enabled'}) as response:

Windows 10, Anaconda 4.4.0, Python 3.5.3, jupyter 4.3.0

issue_title: radio driver : Add a way to configure the value of _RadioDriverThread.TRIES_BEFORE_DISCON
issue_body:
Hello,

When working with multiple drones (5) without using broadcast packets, I got drones randomly disconnecting.

This problem was due to packet losses, and the drone being disconnected by [this piece of code](https://github.com/bitcraze/crazyflie-lib-python/blob/master/cflib/crtp/radiodriver.py#L492)

I tried hardcoding `_RadioDriverThread.TRIES_BEFORE_DISCON` to extremely high values, and my problem disapeared, and the drones were still responsive.

It would be great, though, if there was a method call to set this value directly. I did not make a PR as it seems to need some non-trivial changes in order not to be too *hacky*.

If an `rrule` is created with timezone information in `DTSTART`, it appears to function correctly, but converting it back to a `str` removes the timezone information from `DTSTART`.

```python
>>> from dateutil.rrule import rrulestr
>>> str(rrulestr('DTSTART:20180102T120000Z\nFREQ=DAILY'))
'DTSTART:20180102T120000\nFREQ=DAILY'
```

Expected output:
`DTSTART:20180102T120000Z\nFREQ=DAILY`

OS: Alpine Linux 3.7
dateutil version 2.6.1
Python 3.6.3

I am using RRULE strings to store date/time information in a database. For now, I'm working around this by managing `DTSTART` separately from the RRULE strings and stripping it when serializing/deserializing the RRULE.

issue_title: Update guided mode moving example and guide for MAV_FRAME_BODY_OFFSET_NED
issue_body:
The existing guided-set-speed-yaw-demo example documentation around vehicle movement is "mostly" correct. The example uses MAV_FRAME_BODY_NED  and should AFAIK work as expected - navigate relative to the home position.

However the documentation states that the frame is ignored in [SET_POSITION_TARGET_LOCAL_NED](http://dev.ardupilot.com/wiki/copter-commands-in-guided-mode/#set_position_target_local_ned) - from AC 3.3 this is no longer the case. Now you can set position and velocity relative to your vehicle heading etc based on the frame type. This can be very convenient for driving the vehicle!

Upshot, we need to look at the existing example and documentation and see if we can improve it.

### What is wrong?

In the `trinity` module we have a number of path objects that are typed as `str`.  

The `pathlib` standard library module has a [`PurePath`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html#pathlib.PurePath) class which is more appropriate.


### How can it be fixed

Update the `trinity` module to use the `PurePath` type for all filesystem paths.  If it seems appropriate, lets also update the code to actually use and return `PurePath` objects.

* Version: 4.2.1
* Python: 3.6
* OS: osx

### What was wrong?

I have written the following middleware to throw an error when the provider isn't connected. Unfortunately this fails when running killing the ethereum client while running the middleware.

```py
def connection_test_middleware(make_request, web3):
    '''
    Creates middleware that checks if the provider is connected.
    '''
    def middleware(method, params):
        if web3.providers and web3.providers[0]:
            provider = web3.providers[0]

            try:
                if not provider.isConnected():
                    raise EthNodeCommunicationError('Web3 provider not connected')
                else:
                    return make_request(method, params)
        else:
            raise EthNodeCommunicationError('Web3 provider not connected')
    return middleware
```

issue_title: gcloud on appegnine - deadline doesn't work the way it should
issue_body:
Hey,

During my usage in gcloud(which uses httplib2) over appengine environment I got an exceptions when my request to a url took more than 5 seconds. According to google app engine documentation when you need more than 5 seconds for a request you should use: 
`urlfetct.set_default_fetch_deadline(seconds)`
So I did. But the problem exception kept happening. When using fetch, you are passing the deadline parameter by (line 1002):
`socket.getdefaulttimeout() or 5` 
but it should be in this way to support appengine users: 
`deadline = get_default_fetch_deadline() or socket.getdefaulttimeout() or 5`

Attaching my code (I'm not familiar with google3 package) :

`def _new_fixed_fetch(validate_certificate):
        def fixed_fetch(url, payload=None, method="GET", headers={},
                        allow_truncated=False, follow_redirects=True,
                        deadline=None):
            if deadline is None:
                deadline = get_default_fetch_deadline() or socket.getdefaulttimeout() or 5
            return fetch(url, payload=payload, method=method, headers=headers,
                         allow_truncated=allow_truncated,
                         follow_redirects=follow_redirects, deadline=deadline,
                         validate_certificate=validate_certificate)
        return fixed_fetch`

issue_title: replace old style print-s in the examples
issue_body:
Hi,
Great stuff, however the examples are full of old style print-s ("print whatever" instead of "print(whatever)"). These do not work with recent python 3 (plus having used other programming languages these are plain ugly and WTF-ish (not sure why python introduced the old-style print in the first place)). Plus these confuse PyCharm formatting - should you try to copy-paste such code to PyCharm.

I think logging.info() should be the best replacement for print-s. Initialization of logging is easy with logging.basicConfig(), and it would be great opportunity to make it easy to change the log level to DEBUG to see ncclient debug logs if needed while playing with the examples.

I would do a PR for this if needed, and if my proposal is acceptable by the author(s) and/or maintainer(s).

Example for basicConfig:
```
import logging
import sys

LOG_FORMAT = '%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s'
logging.basicConfig(stream=sys.stdout, level=logging.INFO, format=LOG_FORMAT)

logging.info('Hello %s', 'world!')
```

issue_title: Compatability with latest version of oauthlib
issue_body: v1.0.0 was released the other day (and v1.0.1 just came out yesterday). It looks like there are a few "breaking changes"; it's probably a good idea to check over all this to make sure is fully compatible with the new version: https://github.com/idan/oauthlib/releases

Due to a bug on Cython's include resolution(?), it is not possible to conditionally include cysignals depending on the OS: see https://groups.google.com/d/topic/cython-users/E9_hATlRhII/discussion

I wonder whether the devs of cysignals would agree to provide a stub version of cysignals for Windows, where all functions are simply do-nothing: from my point of view, the ability to handle signals is mostly a convenience to the user (albeit a hugely helpful one) and rarely carries semantic weight; however, providing such stubs would make the use of a single code base across OSes much easier.  A quick skimming of the docs suggested that the only function that would behave seriously differently is `alert`, which could e.g. immediately raise a RuntimeError on Windows.  (As a side note, a list of all available functions would be nice for the docs).

Hopefully Cython will fix this issue and the stubs can then be removed...

     $ pip install raven
    ....
      File "/home/chris/git/myproject/.env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/raven/transport/aiohttp.py", line 31
        def __init__(self, parsed_url, *, verify_ssl=True, resolve=True,

### The change consists of 4 elements: - adding a `@Versioned` marker interface - changing the serializer's read and write methods to add `@Versioned` serialization - adding a `getVersion` method in the `ObjectDataInput` and `ObjectDataOutput` - adding support for using the `@Versioned` serialization only if `hazelcast.rolling.upgrade.enabled`set to true (for backward compatibility) ### Full description: Before 3.8 there were 2 DataSerializable binary formats: - DataSerializable (DS) - IdentifiedDataSerializable (IDS) In 3.8 there's a new orthogonal dimensions added, since each of those types may be @Versioned or not. Previously, in order to distinguish if the binary stream being sent is a DS or IDS there was a boolean sent, if `true` it was na IDS, if `false` it was DS. The value sent in the byte stream for that boolean, was byte `1` for true, and `0` for false. In 3.8 this boolean byte has been promoted to a byte so we do `readByte` instead of `readBoolean`. The semantics of that byte is backward-compatible, but it is used as a bitset with the following semantics: ``` /** * The serializaion header consists of one java byte primitive value. * Bits are used in the following way (from the least significant to the most significant) * 0.) 0=data_serializable, 1=identified_data_serializable * 1.) 0=non-versioned, 1=versioned * 2.) 0=uncompressed, 1=compressed (either classId and factoryId or class package name) * 3.) unused * 4.) unused * 5.) unused * 6.) unused * 7.) unused *

* Earlier the header was just a byte holding boolean value: * - 0=data_serializable, 1=identified_data_serializable * thus the new format is fully backward compatible. */ ``` ### Changes in the `@Versioned` serialization format: 1.) if compressed bit set (applies to IDS only) - then the factoryId and classId are sent as single `bytes` not `integers`. It allows to skip the transfer of 6 redundant bytes. 2.) if versioned bit set (applies to both DS and IDS) - then after the factoryId & classId (IDS) or className (DS) there is an extra byte sent which is a `version`. It has to be read too. ### Version in the ObjectDataInput The Version in the ObjectDataInput is a byte received by the client in a transmission from a member. The semantics of the `version` byte received by the client from a member is that it's the `minor` version of the `clusterVersion` that serialized that object. The major version is the same as the client major version, since there's no compatibility among major version changes, so the client has to have the same major version as the cluster. The client should then create a Version object consisting of `major` and `minor` versions and set it on the ObjectDataInput for the sake of the deserialization. The patch version is always zero and is not sent - we do not do versioned serialization on the basis of patch releases. They need to be binary compatible anyway. So having the version the user may do version-aware deserialization of the object, like this: ``` readData(ObjectDataInput in) { if(in.getVersion().equals(Version.of(3,8)) { in.readInt() } in.readByte } ``` ### Version in the ObjectDataOutput Version sent in the ObjectDataOutput is the version of the party that serialized the object. On the client it is the client. The serializer should include the byte version in the byte stream if both conditions are met: - if the `hazelcast.rolling.ugprade.enabled` GroupProperty is set to true - if the DS or IDS class implements the marker `@Versioned` interface Also if both conditions are met the client should set its major.minor version on the ObjectDataOutput so that the user may do conditional serialization of fields while serializing the object (in the same way as presented above on deserialization) ### Other things to consider: - All classes marked as @BinaryInterface have to be compatible among all clients (java and non-java). We need to have a test suite for that. ### How it was done in the EE client In order to see how it was implement in Java, plz have a look at the following classes: - EnterpriseDataSerializableHeader - EnterpriseDataSerializableSerializer - VersionedObjectDataInput - VersionedObjectDataOutput It sounds like a big task, but in reality it's really small. I'll be happy to be the reviewer of this work.

GAE does not support the 'mmap' library in Python. Applications have no direct access to files at the operating system level. try :

issue_title: Revisar y arreglar implementación del writer de XLSX
issue_body:
**Contexto**

La implementación del writer de XLSX debería ser tal que la lectura/escritura entre la representación en XLSX y JSON sea _idempotente_.

Se debería verificar que:

```
catalog = DataJson("sample_catalog.xlsx")

catalog.to_json("saved_data.json")
catalog.to_xlsx("saved_catalog.xlsx")

catalog_json = DataJson("saved_data.json")
catalog_xlsx = DataJson("saved_catalog.xlsx")

catalog_json == catalog_xlsx
```

Y obviamente lo mismo si el ejemplo arranca con `catalog = DataJson("sample_data.json")` o de cualquiera de las otras maneras que se puede leer un catálogo.

**Propuesta**

at least two recent commits that need to go out:

- https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/commit/2cfcb7a3d53f08618b98ff19b609ac98aa5bc528
- https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/commit/5aea7d0de5acbcd2881251facace208009d4afeb

slating this for this week.

As discussed with @illicitonion (and previously on #4289), we will likely want to make some process-specific syntax for rules, since they are guaranteed to need to wrap inputs and outputs (which would otherwise just be Snapshots and Snapshots, or ExecuteProcessRequests and ExecuteProcessResults) in order to disambiguate them from other process executions.

An expanded version of the syntax that we came up with was:
```
@process(ClasspathEntry, [Select(Javac), SelectProjection(Snapshot, SourcesField, 'source_field', Target)])
def javac(binary, sources_snapshot):
  out_dir = "doesnt_matter"
  process_request = SnapshottedProcessRequest(args=(binary.name,
                                                    '-d', out_dir)+
                                                    tuple(f.path for f in sources_snapshot.files),
                                              snapshots=(binary.snapshot, sources_snapshot),
                                              directories_to_create=(out_dir.path,))
  process_result = yield process_request
  if process_result.exit_code:
    raise ...
  return ClasspathEntry(process_result.snapshot.re_root(outdir))
```

I expect that either as-part-of, or immediately-after @ity's current PR, we will want to do something like this. In the meantime though, continuing to have `input_conversion` and `output_conversion` functions would likely be ok: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/4d6427304aa5516a05398593c4d2ba86e44164ff/src/python/pants/engine/isolated_process.py#L150-L167

issue_title: Android examples cannot all be compiled in one run
issue_body:
Before isolated compile, the `unpacked_libraries` and `dx_compile` tasks operated over AndroidLibrary targets. But now they need to be refactored to work over AndroidBinary targets instead. 

Those tasks unpack .aar archives and create AndroidLibrary targets from their contents. This included adding a JarLibrary dependency (from the classes.jar inside the aar archives) to every dependent AndroidLibrary. But those classes.jars are now needed on the classpath of the actual AndroidBinary targets in order to compile. This previously just worked under global_compile strategy, but in a correctness sense those jars should be dependencies of the AndroidBinaries.

I have hacked the tests to go green in an attempt to move android backend to contrib. But if a pants run globs multiple android targets, the graph gets constructed incorrectly and dependencies are missing.

To repro: pants bundle examples/src/android::
To fix: have unpacked_libraries operate on AndroidBinary targets. That change is not so bad but it will also have to be reflected in DxCompile, which filters each unpacked_library based on the include/exclude pattern of the Binary's AndroidLibrary targets. So a major rethink is possibly in order.

issue_title: __init__.py in test modules causing import error
issue_body:
So if I have a test filesystem structure like:
```
- src/python/project/module
  - __init__.py
  - xxx.py
  - yyy.py
- tests/python/project/module
  - __init__.py
  - test_xxx.py
  - test_yyy.py
```
And inside `test_xxx.py` I import some thing from `xxx.py`.  I'll end up getting error import from `xxx.py`.

But if I remove the `__init__.py` from `test/python/project/module` folder, everything works.  I checked `twitter/commons` project and seems like it's not using `__init__.py` in all the test modules.

What's the reason for this behavior and could we at least document this behavior in the official Pants docs?  It took me hours to figure out this behavior.  Thanks.

issue_title: Deprecate `--ivy-soft-excludes` option and make it the default
issue_body:
`--ivy-soft-excludes` is not the default, but it makes "mid-graph" excludes act as one would expect: namely, that they only apply to the portion of the graph that they affect.

Twitter has been using `--ivy-soft-exlcludes` for at least a year, and it still confuses people occasionally upstream due to not being the default.

issue_title: There is no primitive for marking an option / attribute as never cacheable
issue_body:
Currently there are primitives for marking options as fingerprintable and adding target attributes to a fingerprint. There is no such primitive for marking an option or attribute value as a poison to cacheability. An example of such an option is running a task with profiling turned on - the expectation is this sort of run should probably never be cached and a fresh profile should be run. It may make sense to wrap up a sane means of correctly marking an input set uncacheable.

Discovered working #4587 when adding caching to pytest runs where `--profile` is a test run option to turn on profiling.

issue_title: Letsencrypt cert not accepted by all browsers
issue_body:
After installing my new cert, I tested with several browsers. Chrome and Firefox on Ubuntu were happy. Safari and Chrome on OSX were happy. Chrome on iOS was happy.

But Firefox on OSX, Safari on iOS and all three Android browsers (the native client, Chrome and Firefox) all complained about the cert.

Is this to be expected, or have I done something wrong?

issue_title: bad syntax if da/dv items are not numerically sorted
issue_body:
When I want to download some items, mpsyt throws a "bad syntax" error if the items after a da or dv command are not sorted numerically. 

Let's say I want to download videos # 1, 3 and 11 in a search results list:

`da 1,3,11` works fine

`da 11,1,3` causes the "bad syntax" error

issue_title: Piping output to input of os command causes unhandled exception on Python 3.x
issue_body:
The output redirection feature where command output can be piped (|) as input to operating-system commands, as in "help | wc" is broken for Python 3.x but still works for Python 2.7.  This feature's expected behavior is documented here:
https://cmd2.readthedocs.io/en/latest/freefeatures.html#output-redirection

Not only is it broken, but it causes an unhandled exception.  It appears to be a str (unicode) vs bytes issue.

When "debug" is set to True, this is the exception I got:
(Cmd) help | wc
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/toddleonhardt/src/cmd2/cmd2.py", line 872, in onecmd_plus_hooks
    stop = self.onecmd(statement)
  File "/Users/toddleonhardt/src/cmd2/cmd2.py", line 955, in onecmd
    stop = func(statement)
  File "/Users/toddleonhardt/src/cmd2/cmd2.py", line 543, in do_help
    cmd.Cmd.do_help(self, arg)
  File "/Users/toddleonhardt/anaconda/lib/python3.5/cmd.py", line 333, in do_help
    self.stdout.write("%s\n"%str(self.doc_leader))
TypeError: a bytes-like object is required, not 'str'

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/toddleonhardt/src/cmd2/cmd2.py", line 877, in onecmd_plus_hooks
    self.restore_output(statement)
  File "/Users/toddleonhardt/src/cmd2/cmd2.py", line 928, in restore_output
    self.kept_state.stdout.write(result or '')
TypeError: write() argument must be str, not bytes

Following the installation instructions on Mac OS X (El Capitan) using the homebrew version of Python, failed to find boost (even after doing `brew install boost`). The issue was that boost had already been installed via MacPorts. This can be verified with something like `port installed "boost*"`.

We solved the problem by removing the MacPorts installation of boost via `sudo port uninstall boost`.

However, rerunning `python setup.py install --user` still failed because CMake had cached some files and was still not finding boost. After removing the ray repository and re-cloning it, the installation succeeded.

issue_title: Actor processes not destroyed after Python ref is released
issue_body:
This can be reproduced with the following script. It will create 100 python processes, but even after the list is de-referenced the processes hang around forever (check `ps -A | grep python`).

```
import ray
import time
import sys

@ray.remote
class Actor(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

ray.init()
refs = [Actor.remote() for _ in range(100)]
time.sleep(5)
refs = None
time.sleep(99999)
```

issue_title: malformed JSON: quotes in a string should be converted
issue_body:
in version 3.4.16

The below is a message in JSON format, which contains a string value for the "reason" key, and this string values has embedded quotes around the number 2 ("2"). 
```
{ "exabgp": "3.4.8", "time": 1466620850, "host" : "miab1", "pid" : "28251", "ppid" : "28221", "counter": 50, "type": "state", "neighbor": { "ip": "10.120.1.1", "address": { "local": "10.101.0.1", "peer": "10.120.1.1"}, "asn": { "local": "65015", "peer": "65015"}, "state": "down", "reason": "out loop, peer reset, message [notification sent (1,0)] error[Message header error / Unspecific / can not decode update message of type "2"]"} }
```
The above json can not be properly deserialized because of the incorrect delimiter expected after the doublequote preceding the 2 character in:

"out loop, peer reset, message [notification sent (1,0)] error[Message header error / Unspecific / can not decode update message of type "2"]"

For objects that must be converted to JSON, consider:
changing or removing quotes
inserting \\ before quotes (better)
using a JSON serializer, such as json.dumps (best)

(also note the incorrect version key,value "exabgp": "3.4.8". I'll log another bug for that.)

issue_title: If we want to use this as a live blog
issue_body:
What is a live blog?

> Live blogs make it easy for readers who are intensely following a news story to know what is the latest information. Those readers don’t need to skim seven paragraphs of background information, as they might do in a traditional news article, to figure out what has changed. The latest information is right there, at the top.

 - [ ]  what you need to know section?
 - [ ]  do we want an option for a breaking news ledeall

                c = None

            if c:
                logger.info( "Starting to poll topics [{}]...".format(topics) )
                while running:
                    try:
                        msg = c.poll()
                        if not msg.error():
                            processor(msg)
                        elif msg.error().code() == kafka.KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF:
                            # End of partition event
                            logger.debug( "{} [{}] reached end at offset {}".format(
                                            msg.topic(), msg.partition(), msg.offset()) )
                        else:
                            logger.debug( "Unknown error [{}]. Quitting...".format(msg.error()) )
                            raise kafka.KafkaException(msg.error())
                    except:
                        running = False
                        logger.error( "Error polling messages." )
                # end while running loop
            else:
                logger.error( "Consumer object missing. Nothing to do!" )

            try:
                c.close() # close connection
            except:
                logger.warn( "Could not close connection (c). Nothing to do!" )

        except Exception, e:
            logger.error( "Error consuming topics [{}]".format(topics) )
            logger.debug( traceback.format_exc() )

        return None
~~~

I leveraged the suggested `_on_assign` syntax suggested in #11 (thanks again) if I decided to request `--from-beginning` upon startup.

~~~
def _on_assign (c, ps):
    """Resets the consumer offset"""
    for p in ps:
        p.offset=-2
    c.assign(ps)
~~~

Any ideas why it disconnected after an hour of inactivity after EOF? Thanks!

issue_title: self=self in lambda expression in method dispatch
issue_body:
Hallo pysimplesoap devs and thanks for a great lib.

We recently wanted to use pysimplesoap for [SoCo](https://github.com/SoCo/SoCo), but we ran into some problems using the method dispatch for "without WSDL" and with the elements listed as args. The problem we have, is that the service we want to connect with, is dependent on a specific order of the elements. This means that we cannot use the keyword form of the call spec. When reading through the code, one discovers that it should be possible to call it with the elements as (tag_name, value) args. But when doing that with the method dispatch, one gets an exception `AttributeError: 'int' object has no attribute 'call'`. I did not save a traceback, but I can re-create the behavior with this test code:

```python
class Foo(object):
    def __getattr__(self, attr):
        return lambda *args, **kwargs: self.call(attr, *args, **kwargs)

    def call(self, attr, *args, **kwargs):
        print "self", self
        print "attr", attr
        print "args", args
        print "kwargs", kwargs

class Bar(object):
    def __getattr__(self, attr):
        return lambda self=self, *args, **kwargs: self.call(attr, *args, **kwargs)

    def call(self, attr, *args, **kwargs):
        print "attr", attr
        print "args", args
        print "kwargs", kwargs

if __name__ == '__main__':
    foo = Foo()
    print "No self=self"
    print "\nargs only"
    foo.fancy_name(1, 2, 3)
    print "\nkwargs only"
    foo.fancy_name(c=4, d=5, e=6)
    print "\nmixed"
    foo.fancy_name(1, 2, 3, c=4, d=5, e=6)

    print "\n\nWith self=self"
    bar = Bar()
    print "\nargs only"
    bar.fancy_name(1, 2, 3)
    print "\nkwargs only"
    bar.fancy_name(c=4, d=5, e=6)
    print "\nmixed"
    bar.fancy_name(1, 2, 3, c=4, d=5, e=6)
```

If we use the `call` method directly with the method name as first arg, it works fine.

At a glance, and the way I understand the lambdas, that something=something syntax is used to lock a lambda call value to the value it had at lambda creation time, but I do not see why that should be necessary here (if it is even possible with mutable objects), or why it should be any different from the WSDL case two lines below.

It may of course just be that I missed something, so I look forward to your input.

If you require the actual traceback, I can re-create it for you.

Regards Kenneth

I am currently performing analyses of relatively large images (2000x2000 px) and attempting a local Otsu thresholding on them. As a part of thresholding, I am running the `rank.otsu` function from `scikit-image` on one of the color channels in the image. 

While this approach worked fine for small images (512x512), for larger images `rank.otsu` is taking around 90-120 seconds. Is that an expected speed or is there a chance this is due to a bug/OS issue?

Installing from source on linux, the headers are installed on /usr/local/include/bullet.

However, headers don't include other headers using bullet/*, they include other headers assuming the path being provided is /usr/local/include/bullet.

For example: on BulletCollision/CollisionDispatch/btCollisionWorld.h, it includes LinearMath/btVector3.h.

But the makefile didn't put the LinearMath directory under /usr/local/include, it put it under /usr/local/include/bullet, so it can't find it.

So what people do is to use -I/usr/local/include/bullet to make it use that directory to look for stuff.

But the thing is, if I were to install bullet into another path, such as /usr/include, which is what a packager manager does, my makefile wouldn't work.

So, what I propose is to make the headers reference instead of LinearMath/btVector3.h, bullet/LinearMath/btVector3.h.

Now if the headers are installed anywhere on the system where its a valid path for looking for headers, the makefile works, no need to add a flag just to compensate for the wrong path on the header.

issue_title: Feature request: secure document creation in the absence of meta.version
issue_body:
Currently, there is a risk of over-writing an existing document if a new object is saved with the same id.

```
>>> test_user = TestUser(_id=1, email="test@email.com")
>>> test_user.save()
True
>>> other_user = TestUser(_id=1, email="other@email.com")
>>> other_user.save()
False
>>> other_user.meta.to_dict()
{'id': '1', 'version': 2, 'index': 'testing'}
>>> check_user = TestUser.get(1)
>>> check_user.to_dict()
{'email': 'other@email.com'}
>>> check_user.meta.to_dict()
{'id': '1', 'doc_type': 'test_users', 'index': 'testing', 'version': 2, 'found': True}
```

My suggestion is to make a save operation automatically add the argument ```op_type='create'```
 in the case where the document has no ```
meta.version```
.

Would that be reasonable?

issue_title: Add compression lun support for Unity
issue_body:
The compression related attributes deprecate since Harrier release:
isCompressionEnabled
compressionSizeSaved
compressionPercent
compressionRatio

The data reduction related attributes is introduced to replace compression attributes:
isDataReductionEnabled
dataReductionSizeSaved
dataReductionPercent
dataReductionRatio

So in the function implementation, we need to consider backward compatibility scenarios.

This is awesome: https://github.com/bgruening/galaxytools/tree/master/workflows/ncbi_blast_plus/find_three_genes_located_nearby

issue_title: Allow passing a message to be set in the TapiocaException through ResponseProcessException

issue_body:

issue_title: zmon security group no longer recognized by postgresapp
issue_body:
The template for the postgresapp uses some assumptions to find the zmon security group.
With the newer zmon appliance, these assumptions are no longer valid.

As the senza yaml files become more and more complex, position base parameters are starting to be quite risky, especially as many params have default values, and one may not notice a change.

It would also be nice to have a senza create --coldrun <name>.yaml that would allow one to see the configured values after the substitutions. 

A standard support for something like senza create <name>.yaml which would show all parameters, and their default values.

We should realize that some stacks are deployed once in a blue moon, and reading the yaml file every time could become cumbersome.

issue_title: Does relay.NodeField support many args more than "id" ?
issue_body:
Hi, guys

In `graphene/relay/fields.py` : 

```python
class NodeField(Field):
    # ...
    def id_fetcher(self, global_id, info):
        # ...
        return object_type.get_node(_id, info)

    def resolver(self, instance, args, info):
        global_id = args.get('id')
        return self.id_fetcher(global_id, info)
```

This define a `get_node` strictly, just global id could be used as arg. 

There is an example about other scene: 

[http://graphql-swapi.parseapp.com](http://graphql-swapi.parseapp.com/?query={%0A%20%20film%28filmID%3A%20%223%22%29%20{%0A%20%20%20%20id%0A%20%20%20%20title%0A%20%20}%0A%20%20allFilms%20{%0A%20%20%20%20edges%20{%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20node%20{%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20id%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20title%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20}%0A%20%20%20%20}%0A%20%20}%0A}%0A)

They define a more useful interface: 

```
film(id: ID, filmID: ID): Film
person(id: ID, personID: ID): Person
planet(id: ID, planetID: ID): Planet
species(id: ID, speciesID: ID): Species
starship(id: ID, starshipID: ID): Starship
vehicle(id: ID, vehicleID: ID): Vehicle
node(id: ID!): Node
```

It has a 'XXXID' arg in every node field. This is just what I wanted. It would be useful where a `NodeField` can queried by other args not just id.

Any help would be appreciated.
Thanks!

Refs:

- [Relay Global Object Identification Specification](https://facebook.github.io/relay/graphql/objectidentification.htm)

Hello,

It seems that python-docx does not have any built in support for '.docm' OOXML documents.  From the perspective of parsing this document type as a docx, there is not much difference: There are some extra document parts that support the macro language / VBA, and the Content Type for the 'word\document.xml' Document Part is 'application/vnd.ms-word.document.macroEnabled.main+xml'.

Attempting to open a '.docm' with python-docx results in the error code "file '...' is not a Word file, content type is 'application/vnd.ms-word.document.macroEnabled.main+xml'", and it does not even attempt to parse the file.

However, because a docm is basically a docx, I can get python-docx reading and writing docm files by adding the 'application/vnd.ms-word.document.macroEnabled.main+xml' Content Type to 'opc/constants.py', assigning the Content Type to a Part Factory in __init__.py, and adding the new Content Type to the gatekeeper check in api.py.

Is this a feature you might add to the library?  So far, it seems like a small change;  I haven't had any issues with my locally patched version yet, though you might know of some gotchas that I haven't encountered yet.

Thank you,

Pete

issue_title: Validate all address input for EIP55 compatibility
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

We are now using web3.py which uses [EIP55](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-55.md) checksummed addresses/.

At the moment we sanitize addresses by using `to_checksum_address()` before passing them on to web3.py.

This is missing the point as we basically don't really validate the checksums on the time of input.

## TODO

- Validate all hex address input by the user for EIP55 compliance
    - Validate hex addresses given through the API endpoints [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/203c77df4e44bac52b5cee8cded2d57c557bbe95/raiden/api/v1/encoding.py#L46)
    - Validate hex addresses given through the cli [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/203c77df4e44bac52b5cee8cded2d57c557bbe95/raiden/utils/cli.py#L199)
~- Remove all `to_checksum_address` uses. If a hex address somehow gets to web3.py without already being checksummed it means we are not sanitizing some input.~

issue_title: New syntax to specify locator type in addition to current `type=value`
issue_body:
Currently when specifying a non-default locator for an element, the syntax is `name=value` like in `xpath=//h1` or `css=h1`. This works well otherwise, but the same syntax is also used by [Robot Framework's named argument syntax](http://robotframework.org/robotframework/latest/RobotFrameworkUserGuide.html#named-argument-syntax) and this can cause confusion.

Using the same syntax also causes actual problems if a keyword would accept `**kwargs`, because then Robot considers all arguments looking like `name=value` to match it. There's also a possibility that some time in the future Robot will always do that, regardless on the function signature. Robot allows escaping the problematic `=` like in `name\=value` (e.g. `css\=h1`) but that is a bit ugly if needed often.

My proposal is that in addition to the existing `=`, we allow using `:` like `name:value` (or `name: value` or even `name : value` as we allow spaces to used with `=` too). The reason I propose `:` is that it's visually quite close to `=`, it's used by Robot on the command like (e.g. `-v name:value`), and it's also used by Python in similarly as `=` in some contexts (e.g.`{'name': 'value'}` and `dict(name=value)`). We don't need to deprecate the current syntax at least now, but documentation should be written so that the new syntax is recommended.

This change causes backwards incompatibility problems if someone has used a default locator (e.g. id) which has `:` in its value `Click Element    foo:bar`. We don't expect `:` to be too common in locator values, and there's always a possibility to use `id:foo:bar` or `id=foo:bar` to avoid the problem. If there are lot of complaints during the SL 3.0 release candidate phase, we can still consider ways to make the transition smoother.

issue_title: Decrypt pgp data chunk by chunk
issue_body:
Will there be an option to decrypt pgp data chunk by chunk?

The use case is, we have a service receiving large pgp file, chunk by chunk. Instead of landing the whole file to local disk (or memory), we want to decrypt the chunks on the fly and pass to 3rd party service.

We're looking for feature similar to how zlib library is decompressing gzip file:

```python
import zlib
decompressobj = zlib.decompressobj(32 + zlib.MAX_WBITS)
return decompressobj.decompress(data_chunk)
```

'decompressobj' consumes the incoming bytes, if the bytes are less than a zipped chunk, it'll return empty string and keep consuming the next piece of bytes.

So can pgp process the encrypted file in similar way?

issue_title: BigQuery: remove circular imports in helper functions that use Row class
issue_body:
With https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/pull/4291 the `Row` class was moved from `_helpers.py` to `table.py`. This caused circular references between the two files, because `_helpers.py` imports `Row` from `Table` and `table.py` imports multiple helper functions from `_helpers.py`.

To temporarily resolve the import errors, the `Row` class was imported within the individual helper functions where it was needed. The circular import issue needs to be resolved so that Row does not have to be imported inside functions.

issue_title: Spanner: timeout for streaming reads is 60 seconds
issue_body:
It seems that the timeout for streaming read operations from Spanner is set to 60 seconds by the Python client. It wasn't clear to me if this was intentional, since the configuration here has `timeout_millis` for `StreamingRead` set to `3600000`: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/26f1a97ec06b5c48df7a3c9eec00ae3985657164/spanner/google/cloud/spanner_v1/gapic/spanner_client_config.py#L66

However, that value is superseded by `initial_rpc_timeout_millis` here: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/26f1a97ec06b5c48df7a3c9eec00ae3985657164/api_core/google/api_core/gapic_v1/config.py#L105

Which for the `default` retry param is `60000`:
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/26f1a97ec06b5c48df7a3c9eec00ae3985657164/spanner/google/cloud/spanner_v1/gapic/spanner_client_config.py#L14

If this is intentional, what's the appropriate way to perform a large-size read out of Spanner using this client?

issue_title: desired_accounts.py logging.info is too chatty in syslog
issue_body:
The code "logging.info('Google SSH key schema identifier found.')" will result in two entries per minute in the syslog which is too spammy like and makes remote-monitoring more complex. 

» 12:15:43.273 rubystackbox-xxx accounts-from-metadata: INFO Google SSH key schema identifier found.
» 12:15:43.400 rubystackbox-xxx accounts-from-metadata: INFO Google SSH key schema identifier found.

When GetForwardedIps runs _RunIpRoute (effective command: ip route ls table local type local scope host dev eth0 proto 66), the result is 0 or more lines. If there is one or more forwarded ips, they are returned one per line but have "local " prepended to them.  This causes ParseForwardedIps to fail.

That may not be the case on other systems, but a simple solution works for all of them.  Before returning from GetForwardedIps, simply do a search & replace:
`result = re.sub(r'local\s((\d{1,3}\.){3}\d{1,3}(/\d{1,2})?)\s?\n', r'\1\n',result)`

then return as normal.  If there is a "local " tag, it gets removed, if not, nothing changes.
This helped me stop thousands of warnings from filling up /var/log/messages with:
  google-ip-forwarding: WARNING Could not parse IP address: "local".

issue_title: GitPython doesn't have an efficient way to extract all information
issue_body:
I'm using GitPython to extract git commit log information from a large repo.  Think 30,000+ commits.

If I do something like this...

```
repo = Repo('...')
commits = list(repo.iter_commits('master'))
for commit in commits:
    yield commit.author.name
```

GitPython will end up calling out to the git once to get the list of commits, and then again for every single commit message.  This makes it take very long. It'd be more efficient if there was a way to instruct GitPython to extract extended commit information from a list of commits initially (perhaps in in `git.rev_list`) instead of having it look up extended information on a single commit by commit basis.  I tried extracting extra information by passing in `format=full` on `iter_commits` but that caused an error in the parsing code.  (`assert len(hexsha) == 40, "Invalid line: %s" % hexsha`)

Is there any way to do this efficiently? I totally understand is this is the sort of thing that GitPython isn't really optimizing for. I'm just resorting back to calling "git log" for now.

issue_title: higher precedence to user assigned positions instead of non-assigned positions
issue_body:
when assigning positions to input field in cwl file, there may occur a case where some inputs are not assigned a position. It seems that all those unassigned fields get 0 position assigned to them. Users usually start assigning position from 1 and thus they come after unassigned fields. Since a user has explicitly mentioned a position, according to me they should get higher precedence than unassigned fields. 
for ex.
```
cwlVersion: v1.0
class: CommandLineTool
baseCommand: echo
inputs:
  example_flag:
    type: boolean
    inputBinding:
      
      prefix: -f
  example_string:
    type: string
    inputBinding:
      position: 1
      prefix: --example-string
  example_int:
    type: int
    inputBinding:
      
      prefix: -i
      separate: false


outputs: [] 
```

should produce(as per me)
` --example-string hello -f -i42
 `
but it produces
` -f -i42 --example-string hello
 `
here input file is:
```
example_flag: true
example_string: hello
example_int: 42
 ```
It may be that its a functionality rather than a bug, in that case we would update documentation and mention this behaviour.

issue_title: Datatable Search does not filter if includes BooleanField
issue_body:
If using the new Django form-like declaration style:

```python
class UploadDatatable(Datatable):

    filename = TextColumn(
        "Filename",
        sources=['filename'])

    upload_user = TextColumn(
        "Upload user",
        sources=['upload_user'])

    upload_datetime = DateTimeColumn(
        "Upload Date",
        sources=['upload_datetime'],
        processor=helpers.format_date("%Y-%m-%d", localize=True))

    sent = BooleanColumn(
        "Sent",
        source=['sent'],
        processor=helpers.make_boolean_checkmark)

    sent_datetime = DateTimeColumn(
        "Sent Date",
        sources=['sent_datetime'],
        processor=helpers.format_date("%Y-%m-%d", localize=True))

    class Meta:
        ordering = ['-id']
        columns = ['id', 'filename', 'upload_datetime', 'upload_user', 'sent', 'sent_datetime']

The Datatable initializes and displays correctly. However, the "Search" does not filter correctly. What I see it is doing is only filtering on the Boolean. In the code the search term for a Boolean is coerced to True or False.

I can get around this by subclassing BooleanField and overriding `prep_search_value`:

```python
class MyBooleanColumn(BooleanColumn):
    model_field_class = models.BooleanField
    handles_field_classes = [models.BooleanField, models.NullBooleanField]
    lookup_types = ('exact', 'in')

    def prep_search_value(self, term, lookup_type):
        return None

Is this a bug or expected behaviors?

issue_title: The library call failed behind proxy.
issue_body:
There is a bug in SUDS library to pick up proxy settings from the environment variables http_proxy & https_proxy. The authentification request use proper proxy but the actual SOAP call doesn't.
Explicit initiation of the client with proxy settings detected by urllib2 does solve the problem.

Pull request for fix [Pass proxy settings to suds library configuration](https://github.com/salesforce-marketingcloud/FuelSDK-Python/pull/79)
